reasoning: The outcome depends on whether the PMMS 30-year fixed rate mortgage average exceeds its 2026-02-26 value of 5.98%. For the March 8 resolution (corresponding to the March 5 PMMS reading), the rate is largely determined by 10-year Treasury yields from the prior week (late February/early March). By February 27, the 10-year Treasury yield had declined slightly to ~4.04%, continuing the downward momentum that drove the PMMS down to 5.98% in the first place. Therefore, a slight decrease or flat reading is more likely than a bounce for the March 5 release, making an increase less than 50% likely (estimated at 40%). For the March 31 resolution (which will capture readings up to March 26), the rate will reflect major upcoming data releases, including the March 6 Non-Farm Payrolls and March 11 CPI report, as well as the March 17-18 FOMC meeting. The greater uncertainty and the potential for mean reversion to the expert consensus forecast (which places 2026 rates in the "low 6% range") pushes the probability closer to 50%. With the current baseline of 5.98% sitting just below this psychological expectation, there is a reasonable possibility of a modest bounce towards 6% if inflation or labor data surprise to the upside. However, given the sustained downward trend of recent weeks, the probability remains slightly skewed against an increase.
